lessons.title: Present continuous for future plans
lessons.description: Use the present continuous tense to talk about fixed, arranged future plans.

content.title: Present continuous for the future
content.body:
We can use the present continuous (am/is/are + -ing) to talk about FIXED future plans â€” things that are already arranged and confirmed.

Examples:
â€¢ I'm meeting Priya at 6 PM tonight. (already arranged)
â€¢ We're flying to Delhi on Saturday. (tickets booked)
â€¢ She's starting her new job on Monday. (confirmed plan)

Key signals: future time words â†’ tonight, tomorrow, next week, on Friday, in the morning
content.grammar_note:
am/is/are + verb-ing + future time expression
This tense = fixed plan, not a prediction

content.title: Negative and question forms
content.body:
Negative: am/is/are NOT + -ing
â€¢ I'm not coming to the party â€” I have an exam.
â€¢ She isn't attending the meeting tomorrow.

Questions: Am/Is/Are + subject + -ing?
â€¢ Are you coming to Meera's wedding?
â€¢ What are you doing this weekend?
â€¢ Is he travelling to Bangalore next week?

Short answers:
â€¢ Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
â€¢ Yes, she is. / No, she isn't.
content.grammar_note:
Question: Are you + verb-ing?
Negative: I'm not + verb-ing

vocabulary.meaning: to plan and organise something in advance
vocabulary.example: We've arranged a team lunch for Friday.

vocabulary.meaning: to make a plan definite and official
vocabulary.example: Can you confirm if you're attending?

vocabulary.meaning: a plan of when things will happen
vocabulary.example: Check the schedule for tomorrow's meetings.

vocabulary.meaning: a fixed time to meet someone
vocabulary.example: I have a dentist appointment on Thursday.

vocabulary.meaning: the place where an event happens
vocabulary.example: The venue for the party is Hotel Raj.

vocabulary.meaning: free and able to do something
vocabulary.example: Are you available on Saturday evening?

sentences: I'm meeting my cousin at the airport tomorrow morning.
We're having a team dinner on Friday evening.
She isn't coming to college next week â€” she's travelling.
Are you doing anything special this weekend?
They're moving to their new flat on the 15th.

content.question: Ravi has booked train tickets to Mysore for Saturday. Which sentence is correct?
options: Ravi travels to Mysore on Saturday.
Ravi is travelling to Mysore on Saturday.
Ravi will travels to Mysore on Saturday.
Ravi travel to Mysore on Saturday.
content.correct: Ravi is travelling to Mysore on Saturday.
content.explanation: A confirmed, booked plan uses present continuous: IS + travelling.

content.question: Priya wants to ask her friend about Saturday plans. Which question is correct?
options: Do you come to the wedding on Saturday?
Are you coming to the wedding on Saturday?
Will you coming to the wedding on Saturday?
You are coming to the wedding on Saturday?
content.correct: Are you coming to the wedding on Saturday?
content.explanation: Future arrangement question: Are + you + verb-ing?

content.question: Arjun's calendar shows 'Doctor - 4 PM Tuesday'. Which is correct?
options: Arjun sees the doctor on Tuesday.
Arjun is seeing the doctor at 4 PM on Tuesday.
Arjun will saw the doctor on Tuesday.
Arjun seeing the doctor at 4 PM Tuesday.
content.correct: Arjun is seeing the doctor at 4 PM on Tuesday.
content.explanation: A booked appointment = fixed future plan â†’ present continuous.

content.question: Which sentence uses present continuous for the FUTURE correctly?
options: I am eating breakfast right now.
She is presenting at the conference next month.
They are watching a movie at the moment.
content.correct: She is presenting at the conference next month.
content.explanation: 'Next month' is a future time signal â€” this is a fixed plan. The others describe actions happening now.

content.title: Weekend plans â€” friends in Bangalore
content.setting: Kavya and Sonal are colleagues in Bangalore catching up about their weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Sonal, do you have any plans this weekend?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Yes! I'm attending my cousin's engagement on Saturday. The function is in Koramangala.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Oh wow, how exciting! Are you going alone?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: No, my whole family is coming. My parents are arriving from Pune on Friday night.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That's lovely. What are you doing on Sunday?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm resting at home in the morning, and then I'm meeting a friend from college for coffee in the evening.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Sounds like a great weekend! I'm visiting the new exhibition at Cubbon Park on Sunday.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh nice! Are you going with anyone?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, I'm going with my neighbour Meera. We've been planning it for two weeks â€” we already have the tickets.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: What time are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: We're meeting there at 11 AM. Are you working late tonight? We could have chai before leaving.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm finishing at 6 today. Yes, let's do that!

comprehension_questions.question: What is Sonal attending on Saturday?
When are Sonal's parents arriving?
What time are Kavya and Meera meeting at the exhibition?

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: I am meet my manager at 3 PM tomorrow.
sentences.correct: I am meeting my manager at 3 PM tomorrow.
sentences.explanation: Present continuous = am/is/are + verb-ING. 'Meet' must become 'meeting'.

sentences.wrong: Are you come to the party on Friday?
sentences.correct: Are you coming to the party on Friday?
sentences.explanation: Question form: Are + you + verb-ING.

sentences.wrong: She is not attend the training next week.
sentences.correct: She is not attending the training next week.
sentences.explanation: Negative: is not + verb-ING (attending).

content.scenario: Your manager asks if you can join a meeting on Tuesday at 2 PM. You already have a confirmed dentist appointment at that exact time. What do you say?
options: I will go to the dentist maybe on Tuesday.
I'm seeing my dentist at 2 PM on Tuesday â€” I already have that appointment. Can we do a different time?
I see the dentist on Tuesday.
content.correct: I'm seeing my dentist at 2 PM on Tuesday â€” I already have that appointment. Can we do a different time?
content.explanation: A booked appointment = present continuous for future. The response is polite and suggests an alternative.

content.instruction: Talk about your real or imagined plans for this coming weekend. Use the present continuous for future and future time expressions.
content.prompt: What are you doing this weekend? Describe at least 4 arranged plans using present continuous.
content.example_response: This weekend is quite busy for me! On Saturday morning, I'm going to the gym with my friend Ravi â€” we've been planning it since last week. In the afternoon, I'm helping my mother shop for the upcoming festival. She wants to buy new clothes and some decoration items, so we're going to T. Nagar. On Saturday evening, I'm watching a movie with my cousins â€” we've already bought the tickets online for the 7 PM show. On Sunday, I'm not doing anything very special in the morning, just relaxing at home. But in the afternoon, I'm meeting my college friend Ananya for lunch at a restaurant near my place. We're catching up after almost three months.

content.instruction: Imagine you are a very busy person next week. Describe your week using present continuous for future plans â€” at least one plan per day.
content.prompt: Describe a busy week ahead using present continuous. Include: who you are meeting, where you are going, and what you are doing each day.
content.example_response: Next week is going to be very hectic! On Monday, I'm presenting the monthly report to my manager at 10 AM. I've already prepared the slides. On Tuesday, I'm having lunch with a client from Mumbai who is visiting our Bangalore office. On Wednesday, I'm attending a digital marketing workshop all day â€” my company has enrolled me. Thursday is slightly free â€” I'm only having a team check-in at 4 PM. But on Friday, I'm flying to Chennai for a family event. My cousin is getting married on Saturday. My flight is at 6 AM, so I'm waking up at 4! On Saturday afternoon, I'm attending the wedding ceremony, and on Sunday, I'm coming back to Bangalore in the evening.

content.instruction: Complete Priya's message to her friend using present continuous for future.
content.text:
Hi Meera!

Just wanted to update you on my plans. I ___ (1/not work) this Friday â€” I took a leave. On Friday morning, I ___ (2/go) to the art gallery with my sister. We already bought the tickets! In the evening, I ___ (3/join) the family for dinner at Grandma's house. On Saturday, my friend Riya ___ (4/visit) from Hyderabad â€” she ___ (5/stay) with us for two days. We ___ (6/take) her to some good restaurants in Chennai since it's her first visit. Are you free on Sunday? What ___ you ___ (7/do)?

content.title: Lesson 1 complete â€” Present continuous for future
key_points: Use am/is/are + -ing for FIXED, ARRANGED future plans
Always use a future time expression: tonight, tomorrow, next week, on Friday
Question: Are you + verb-ing + time?
Negative: I'm not / She isn't + verb-ing
This tense shows the plan is confirmed â€” not just a thought or prediction
content.next_lesson: Going to â€” review and deepen

lessons.title: Going to â€” review and deepen
lessons.description: Master 'going to' for decided plans and predictions based on present evidence. Build on A1 knowledge with more complex uses.

content.title: Going to â€” two main uses
content.body:
GOING TO has two important uses:

1. DECIDED PLANS (you already decided before speaking)
â€¢ I'm going to apply for a new job this year.
â€¢ We're going to repaint the living room next month.
â€¢ She's going to study medicine.

2. PREDICTIONS based on evidence (you can see something now)
â€¢ Look at those clouds â€” it's going to rain!
â€¢ He's not wearing a helmet â€” he's going to fall!
â€¢ The traffic is terrible â€” we're going to be late.
content.grammar_note:
am/is/are + going to + base verb
Evidence predictions: you can SEE the reason right now

content.title: Going to negative and questions
content.body:
Negative: am/is/are NOT going to + base verb
â€¢ I'm not going to waste my time on this.
â€¢ She isn't going to join that company.
â€¢ They aren't going to make it on time.

Questions: Am/Is/Are + subject + going to + base verb?
â€¢ Are you going to attend the training?
â€¢ What are you going to do after college?
â€¢ Is she going to accept the offer?

Short answers:
â€¢ Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
â€¢ Yes, she is. / No, she isn't.
content.grammar_note:
Negative: NOT going to + base verb
Question: Are you going to + base verb?

vocabulary.example: I intend to finish this course by December.
We're aiming to launch the app next quarter.
She's going to apply for the MBA programme.
He's going to resign from his current position.

vocabulary.meaning: to move something to a later time
vocabulary.example: They're going to postpone the event.
I'm definitely going to save more this year.

sentences: I'm going to start learning Python next month.
Look at those clouds â€” it's going to pour!
Are you going to apply for the promotion?

content.question: Arjun has decided to quit his job and start his own business. Which sentence is correct?
options: Arjun will quit his job tomorrow.
Arjun is going to start his own business.
Arjun is starting his own business tomorrow.
content.correct: Arjun is going to start his own business.
content.explanation: A decision already made before speaking = GOING TO. (Note: present continuous would also be possible if a date was arranged, but GOING TO is more natural for a decision without a fixed date.)

content.question: You see your friend walking fast toward a wet floor without noticing the warning sign. What do you shout?
content.correct: He's going to slip!
content.explanation: You can see the evidence right now â€” wet floor + he's not stopping = prediction based on evidence â†’ GOING TO.

content.question: Which sentence shows a DECIDED PLAN using 'going to'?
options: I think it might rain later.
I'm going to visit my grandparents during Diwali â€” I already bought the ticket.
Look â€” she's going to drop that tray!
I will help you if you need me.
content.correct: I'm going to visit my grandparents during Diwali â€” I already bought the ticket.
content.explanation: A pre-decided plan = GOING TO. The other options are prediction (might), evidence prediction, or will-offer.

content.question: Kavya says: 'The baby looks really tired â€” she ___ fall asleep soon.' Which fits?
content.correct: is going to
content.explanation: Visible evidence (baby looks tired) â†’ prediction with GOING TO.

content.title: New Year resolutions â€” Chennai
content.setting: Priya and her friend Arjun are talking about their plans and resolutions for the coming year.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Priya, it's almost the new year! Do you have any big plans or resolutions?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes, quite a few! I'm going to start a savings plan â€” I'm going to put aside 20% of my salary every month.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's very disciplined! I've been meaning to do that too. Any other plans?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm going to apply for a certification course in data analytics. It starts in February.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Wow, you're serious! What about fitness? Are you going to join a gym or something?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm not going to join a gym â€” it didn't work for me last time. But I'm going to start running in the mornings at Besant Nagar beach.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's a great idea! What about travel? Are you going to go anywhere?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes! My cousin and I are going to go to Coorg in March. We already found a lovely homestay. What about you?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'm going to focus on my career this year. I'm going to look for better opportunities â€” maybe in Hyderabad or Pune.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh, are you going to leave Chennai?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Maybe. Look at my current situation â€” the company isn't growing, my salary hasn't increased in two years. Something is going to have to change.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I understand. I hope you find something great, Arjun. This year is going to be better for both of us â€” I can feel it!

There is no gym near her.
comprehension_questions.correct: It didn't work for her last time.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: I'm going to studying for the exam this weekend.
sentences.correct: I'm going to study for the exam this weekend.
sentences.explanation: GOING TO + BASE VERB (no -ing). 'Study', not 'studying'.

sentences.wrong: Look â€” the glass is going to fell!
sentences.correct: Look â€” the glass is going to fall!
sentences.explanation: Base verb after GOING TO: 'fall', not 'fell'.

sentences.wrong: Are they going to comes to the event?
sentences.correct: Are they going to come to the event?
sentences.explanation: GOING TO + BASE VERB: 'come', not 'comes'.

content.scenario: You see your younger brother about to pour a full glass of water with a broken glass. What do you say?
options: You're going to break it â€” that glass is cracked!
content.correct: You're going to break it â€” that glass is cracked!
content.explanation: You can see the evidence (cracked glass + pouring water) â†’ GOING TO for evidence prediction.

content.instruction: What are your personal goals and plans for the next six months? Talk about decided plans using GOING TO.
content.prompt: Share 5 or more things you ARE GOING TO do in the next 6 months. Be specific.
content.example_response: In the next six months, I have several plans I've already decided on. First, I'm going to complete an online English communication course â€” I've already enrolled. I'm also going to save money to buy a new laptop by June. I've already started putting money aside each month. I'm going to cut down on eating out â€” I spend way too much on food delivery. I'm not going to order food online more than twice a week. I'm going to read at least one book every month â€” I've already bought three books to start with. And finally, I'm going to repair my relationship with my cousin Ravi â€” we haven't spoken for six months and that's silly. I'm going to call him this weekend.

content.instruction: Look around you or imagine a scene. Make 4 predictions about what is GOING TO happen based on what you can see.
content.prompt: Describe 4 evidence-based predictions using 'going to'. Use phrases like: 'look at...', 'I can see that...', 'the sky is...'
content.example_response: Let me look around and make some predictions! I can see that the sky outside is getting very dark and cloudy â€” it's going to rain heavily in the next hour. My phone battery is at 3% and I haven't found a charger â€” it's going to die very soon. My neighbour has been playing loud music for 3 hours and I can hear other neighbours knocking on the wall â€” I think there's going to be an argument. And I can see my younger sister hasn't studied at all for her exam tomorrow â€” she's going to have a very tough time! These are all things that seem very likely based on what I can observe right now.

content.instruction: Complete this New Year message using GOING TO.
content.text:
Happy New Year, everyone!

This year, our team ___ (1/focus) on three main goals. First, we ___ (2/launch) the upgraded version of our app by March â€” the development team has already started. Second, we ___ (3/expand) to two new cities â€” Hyderabad and Pune. We have already shortlisted office spaces. Third, we ___ (4/not/repeat) the mistakes of last year â€” we ___ (5/improve) our customer support system significantly. I ___ (6/personally/meet) each team every month to ensure we stay on track. Together, we ___ (7/make) this our best year yet!

content.title: Lesson 2 complete â€” Going to
key_points: GOING TO use 1: Decided plans made before speaking
GOING TO use 2: Predictions based on evidence you can see NOW
Form: am/is/are + going to + BASE VERB
Negative: am/is/are NOT going to + base verb
Question: Are you going to + base verb?
Key difference: Present continuous = arranged date/time; Going to = decision or visible evidence
content.next_lesson: Will â€” predictions and spontaneous decisions

lessons.title: Will â€” predictions and spontaneous decisions
lessons.description: Use 'will' for predictions about the future, spontaneous decisions, offers, and promises.

content.title: Will â€” four main uses
content.body:
WILL + base verb has four important uses:

1. PREDICTIONS (general, based on belief/knowledge)
â€¢ Electric vehicles will be everywhere in 10 years.
â€¢ She'll be a great doctor â€” she works so hard.

2. SPONTANEOUS DECISIONS (decided at the moment of speaking)
â€¢ [Phone rings] I'll get it!
â€¢ [Friend is struggling] I'll help you carry that.

3. OFFERS
â€¢ I'll make tea. You rest.
â€¢ Shall I carry that for you? / I'll carry that.

4. PROMISES
â€¢ I'll call you as soon as I reach.
â€¢ I won't tell anyone â€” I promise.
content.grammar_note:
will + base verb (all persons: I/you/he/she/we/they)
Short form: I'll, you'll, she'll, we'll, they'll
Negative: won't (= will not)

content.title: Will â€” questions and negative
content.body:
Questions with WILL:
â€¢ Will you be at the office tomorrow?
â€¢ Will it rain this evening?
â€¢ Will she accept the offer?

Negative â€” WON'T:
â€¢ I won't be late, I promise.
â€¢ They won't finish by Friday at this rate.
â€¢ He won't tell you â€” he never talks about personal things.

Shall I / Shall we? (offers/suggestions, British English)
â€¢ Shall I open the window?
â€¢ Shall we order food?
content.grammar_note:
Question: Will + subject + base verb?
Negative: won't / will not + base verb

vocabulary.meaning: to say what you think will happen
vocabulary.example: Experts predict prices will rise.

vocabulary.meaning: to say you will definitely do something
vocabulary.example: I promise I'll return the book.

vocabulary.meaning: to say you are willing to do something for someone
vocabulary.example: She offered to help with the project.
He refused to share the data.
It'll probably be cold in Shimla.
She'll definitely get the job â€” she's brilliant.

sentences: Don't worry â€” I'll help you with the presentation.
She'll probably be promoted by the end of the year.
I won't tell anyone, I promise.
Will you be free for a call at 3 PM?
Technology will change everything in the next decade.

content.question: Your friend drops her bag and things fall everywhere. You decide at that moment to help. What do you say?
options: I am going to help you.
I'll help you pick those up!
I was going to help you.
content.correct: I'll help you pick those up!
content.explanation: A decision made at the moment of speaking = WILL. You decided just now, not before.

content.question: Meera wants to make a promise to her mother. Which is correct?
options: I'm going to call you every day, Amma.
I will call you every day, Amma.
I call you every day, Amma.
I'm calling you every day, Amma.
content.correct: I will call you every day, Amma.
content.explanation: Promises use WILL. Both options A and B are possible, but WILL is the classic promise structure.

content.question: Which sentence is a GENERAL PREDICTION using will?
options: I'll get the door â€” I'm nearest.
I'm meeting my boss at 2 PM.
Renewable energy will replace fossil fuels in the coming decades.
I'm going to apply for the visa next week.
content.correct: Renewable energy will replace fossil fuels in the coming decades.
content.explanation: A general, opinion-based prediction about the future = WILL.

content.question: Your colleague looks tired and is struggling to carry heavy boxes. You want to offer help. Which is the most natural?
options: You are going to need help.
Shall I help you carry those?
content.correct: Shall I help you carry those?
content.explanation: 'Shall I...?' is a natural offer in English. 'I'll help you carry those' is also correct and common.

content.answer: I won't be late, I promise.

content.title: Helping a friend move flats â€” Mumbai
content.setting: Ravi is moving to a new flat in Mumbai. His friend Sonal calls to offer help.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Ravi! I heard you're moving this weekend. Do you need any help?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Yes! It's going to be a lot of work. The new flat is on the 5th floor and there's no lift.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'll come and help you on Saturday. What time do you want to start?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: We're starting at 9 AM. Are you sure? It'll be a lot of carrying.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm sure! I'll bring Ankit too â€” he won't say no if I ask him nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: That's so kind of you both. I'll organise lunch for everyone. What do you want?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Don't worry about that â€” I'll order biryani for everyone. It'll be my contribution.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: You're amazing. I promise I'll help you the next time you move!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Ha! I won't hold you to that. Will your family come too?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: My brother will come in the afternoon. He's busy in the morning. It'll go faster with more people.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: It will definitely be done by evening. Don't worry!

dialogue.speaker: Ravi

key_points: WILL use 1: General predictions (based on belief/knowledge)
WILL use 2: Spontaneous decisions (decided at the moment of speaking)
WILL use 3: Offers (I'll help / Shall I...?)

Form: will + BASE VERB (no change for any person)

lessons.title: Three futures compared
lessons.description: Understand and choose correctly between will, going to, and present continuous for future meaning.

content.title: Three ways to talk about the future
content.body:
English has three common ways to express the future:

1. WILL + base verb
   â†’ Predictions (no evidence), spontaneous decisions, offers, promises
   'I think it will rain.' / 'I'll get the door!'

2. GOING TO + base verb
   â†’ Decided plans, evidence-based predictions
   'I'm going to study medicine.' / 'Look â€” it's going to fall!'

3. PRESENT CONTINUOUS (am/is/are + -ing)
   â†’ Fixed, arranged plans with a time/date
   'I'm meeting her at 6 tonight.' / 'We're flying to Delhi on Monday.'
content.grammar_note:
Will = no plan before / just decided / belief
Going to = decision before / visible evidence
Present continuous = arranged date & time already

content.title: Choosing the right future â€” quick guide
content.body:
Use this guide to decide:

Q: Did you decide JUST NOW? â†’ WILL
â€¢ 'The phone's ringing.' â†’ 'I'll get it!'

Q: Did you decide BEFORE speaking? â†’ GOING TO
â€¢ 'I decided last week to save more money.' â†’ 'I'm going to save more.'

Q: Is it a FIXED APPOINTMENT with a date/time? â†’ PRESENT CONTINUOUS
â€¢ 'I have a booked meeting slot.' â†’ 'I'm meeting the client at 3.'

Q: Is it a GENERAL PREDICTION (no evidence)? â†’ WILL
â€¢ 'I think electric cars will be normal in 10 years.'

Q: Can you SEE the evidence right now? â†’ GOING TO
â€¢ 'The sky is black.' â†’ 'It's going to storm!'
content.grammar_note: When in doubt: Is there a specific time/date? â†’ Present continuous. Decided before? â†’ Going to. Just now? â†’ Will.

vocabulary.example: I'll be with you in a moment.
The train will arrive shortly.
I'll finish it by the end of today.

vocabulary.example: I'll visit Japan at some point.
It will pay off in the long run.
Sooner or later, things will improve.

I think things will get better â€” don't worry.
She's going to resign â€” she told me last night.
The bag is too heavy â€” it's going to break!
I'll carry that for you â€” put it down.

content.instruction: Think of 6 sentences about the future â€” 2 using will, 2 using going to, and 2 using present continuous. Then explain WHY you chose each form.
content.prompt: Create 2 examples each of will, going to, and present continuous for future. Explain your choice.
content.example_response:
Let me give you six future sentences with my reasons.

Using WILL: 'I think electric vehicles will be the standard within 10 years' â€” this is my general prediction based on current trends, not specific evidence I can see right now. 'The phone is ringing â€” I'll get it!' â€” I decided this just now, spontaneously.

Using GOING TO: 'I'm going to start a YouTube channel this year â€” I've already bought the camera' â€” this is a plan I decided before speaking and I have evidence I'm serious. 'Look at that ice cream melting in the sun â€” it's going to drip everywhere!' â€” I can see the evidence happening right now.

Using PRESENT CONTINUOUS: 'I'm attending my sister's graduation ceremony on Saturday at 2 PM' â€” fixed, booked event with a specific time. 'We're having a team offsite on Friday â€” the hotel is booked and everyone confirmed' â€” fully arranged plan.

content.instruction: You are a career counsellor speaking to a student who wants advice about their future. Ask and answer questions about their plans using all three future forms naturally.
content.prompt: Role-play as yourself or a student: describe your future plans, predictions, and arrangements using all 3 future forms.
content.example_response: As a career counsellor, I would first ask: 'So, what are you planning to do after finishing your degree?' And my student might say: 'I'm going to take a break for a few months â€” I've already decided. I need time to think.' Then I'd ask about specific steps: 'Have you arranged anything for that period?' And they might say: 'Yes, I'm doing a 2-month internship at a design agency â€” starts on June 1st.' Then for bigger predictions: 'Where do you see yourself in 5 years?' â€” 'I think I'll probably work in the creative industry. I believe good designers will always have opportunities.' At the end of the session, I'd make an offer: 'I'll send you some resources about portfolio building this evening â€” check your email.'

PRESENT CONTINUOUS: fixed, arranged plan with specific time/date

lessons.title: May & might â€” possibility
lessons.description: Use may and might to talk about things that are possible but not certain in the future.

content.title: May and might â€” expressing possibility
content.body:
We use MAY and MIGHT to talk about things that are POSSIBLE â€” not certain.

MAY = slightly more certain (about 50% chance)
MIGHT = slightly less certain (about 30-40% chance)
In everyday speech, they are often interchangeable.

Examples:
â€¢ I may go to the party â€” I haven't decided yet.
â€¢ It might rain this evening â€” carry an umbrella.
â€¢ She might get the job â€” she interviewed well.
â€¢ We may not make it on time â€” the traffic is bad.

Negative:
â€¢ I may not come tomorrow.
â€¢ He might not know the answer.
content.grammar_note:
may/might + BASE VERB (no 'to', no -ing)
Negative: may not / might not (mayn't is rare; mightn't is informal)
No 's' for he/she/it â€” may/might never change form

content.title: May/might vs will vs going to
content.body:
Compare these levels of certainty:

100% certain:
'I will be there at 6.' (definite)

High certainty (decided plan):
'I'm going to be there at 6.' (already decided)

Possible (not certain):
'I may/might be there at 6.' (not sure yet)

Key tip: When you are NOT SURE if something will happen, use MAY or MIGHT â€” not WILL.

Wrong: 'I will maybe go.' âœ—
Right: 'I might go.' âœ“
Right: 'I may go.' âœ“
content.grammar_note:
will = certain prediction / decided
going to = decided plan / evidence
may/might = possible, not certain

content.explanation: MAY = possible but not certain â€” exactly what 'not 100% sure but good chance' means.

content.question: What is the difference between 'I might come' and 'I'm coming'?
options: No difference â€” both mean the same thing.
'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
'I might come' = very certain. 'I'm coming' = uncertain.
'I might come' is past tense. 'I'm coming' is present tense.
content.correct: 'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
content.explanation: This is a key distinction: might = uncertain possibility; present continuous for future = confirmed arrangement.

content.title: Planning a trip with uncertainty â€” Pune
content.setting: Kavya and her friend Ravi are trying to plan a trip to Himachal Pradesh but are unsure about many things.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ravi, I've been thinking about that Himachal trip we discussed. Do you think it'll actually happen this year?

dialogue.text: It might! I may be able to take leave in October, but it depends on the project deadline.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: October sounds good â€” the weather might be perfect there. But what if it snows already?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: It may snow in higher areas like Spiti, but Manali and Kasol might still be okay in October.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Should we book early? Or might prices come down closer to the date?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: True. What about Sonal â€” might she join us?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: She may come. She said she might take a long break in October, but she may not be able to afford it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I see. And transport â€” should we rent a car?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: We might rent a car or we may just take cabs from Manali. Depends on the group size.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Okay, so nothing is confirmed yet?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Not yet! But it might all come together by next month. Let's decide by August.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya

content.instruction: Talk about 5 things in your life that are POSSIBLE but not certain in the next few months. Use may, might, may not, and might not.
content.prompt: Share things that might happen for you soon â€” plans, changes, possibilities you're considering.
content.example_response: There are several things in my life right now that are possible but not confirmed. First, I might get a transfer to our Hyderabad office â€” my manager mentioned it but nothing is decided. I may apply for a part-time online course in data science â€” I've been thinking about it for months but I'm not sure about the time commitment. My family might move to a bigger flat â€” we've been looking but haven't found the right place yet. My friend and I may go on a road trip to Coorg in September, but it depends on whether we both get leave approved at the same time. And I might finally start that YouTube channel I've been planning â€” I may not though, because I'm still a bit nervous about putting myself out there.

content.instruction: A friend asks you 5 questions about next week. Answer each one using may or might â€” because you're genuinely not certain about any of them.
content.prompt: Answer these 5 questions with uncertain responses: 1. Are you free next Saturday? 2. Will you come to the team lunch? 3. Will it be a busy week for you? 4. Are you going to the gym next week? 5. Will you be in the office on Friday?
content.example_response: Great questions! Let me answer honestly. Am I free next Saturday? I might be free â€” it depends on whether my cousin's visit from Coimbatore happens or not. She may come this weekend or she may postpone. Will I come to the team lunch? I may come if I finish my morning deliverables on time. I might be in back-to-back calls until 2 PM though, so it depends. Will it be a busy week? It might be very busy actually â€” we have a client review on Wednesday which might run long. Am I going to the gym? I may go on Tuesday and Thursday, but I might skip if work gets too hectic. And will I be in the office on Friday? I might work from home on Friday â€” my manager may allow it. So the honest answer to all five is: it depends!

content.title: Hedging language â€” sounding natural with uncertainty
content.body:
In natural conversation, we don't always speak with 100% certainty. We use 'hedging' language to soften what we say.

Common hedging expressions:
â€¢ I think... (mild opinion)
â€¢ I'm not sure but... (uncertainty)
â€¢ It's possible that... (formal possibility)
â€¢ There's a chance that... (possibility)
â€¢ I suppose... (reluctant agreement or mild thought)
â€¢ Probably / possibly / perhaps (adverbs of possibility)
â€¢ As far as I know... (based on my limited information)
â€¢ I imagine... (I believe but am not certain)
â€¢ Don't quote me on this, but... (informal â€” I'm not 100% sure)
content.grammar_note:
These phrases come BEFORE the main statement
Example: 'I think it might rain.' = double hedging (both think + might)

content.title: Responding to uncertain plans
content.body:
When someone gives an uncertain answer (with may/might), you can respond naturally:

Accepting uncertainty:
â€¢ 'No worries â€” let me know when you decide.'
â€¢ 'That's fine â€” just keep me updated.'
â€¢ 'Let's play it by ear.' (decide later based on how things go)

Asking for more info:
â€¢ 'What does it depend on?'
â€¢ 'Do you have a rough idea yet?'
â€¢ 'When do you think you'll know for sure?'

Keeping options open:
â€¢ 'Either way works for me.'
â€¢ 'I'll pencil you in for now.'
content.grammar_note:
'Let's play it by ear' = decide as things develop
'Pencil in' = put in the schedule but not final

content.instruction: Someone asks you about the future of your industry or career field over the next 10 years. Give a hedged, thoughtful response using possibility language.
content.prompt: Talk about what might happen in your industry or career field in the next 10 years. Use hedging language throughout.
content.example_response: I work in the IT sector, so there are quite a few things I think might change in the next 10 years. First, I imagine AI will probably take over a lot of routine coding tasks â€” things like testing and debugging might become largely automated. I suppose that could be good or bad depending on your role. As far as I know, companies are already investing heavily in AI tools. There's a strong possibility that cloud computing will become even more central â€” most businesses may move entirely off on-premise servers. I think cybersecurity might become one of the most important and well-paid fields. It's also possible that remote work will remain common â€” maybe even more so. I don't think jobs in tech will disappear entirely though. There'll probably always be a need for creative problem-solvers. I suppose the key is to keep learning and adapting â€” whatever happens.

content.instruction: Describe your ideal but uncertain future. Where might you be living in 5 years? What might your life look like? Use lots of hedging language.
content.prompt: Talk about where you might be and what your life might look like in 5 years. Be honest about what's uncertain.
content.example_response: In five years, I honestly don't know exactly where I'll be â€” and I think that's okay! I suppose I might still be in Chennai, or I might have moved to Bangalore or even abroad for work. As far as I know, there are good opportunities growing in Hyderabad too, so that's also possible. I imagine I'll probably have advanced in my career â€” maybe into a senior or lead role. I think I might be working in a hybrid setup, partly from home and partly in an office. There's a chance I may have started a side project or small business by then â€” I've been thinking about it for a while. On the personal side, I might be married, I might not be â€” it depends on how things go. I'd like to probably travel more â€” maybe visit Japan or Europe at some point. I suppose the honest answer is: a lot might change, and I'm open to most of it!

lessons.title: Writing formal emails â€” structure and language
lessons.description: Learn how to write clear, polite formal emails in English â€” greeting, purpose, body, and sign-off.

content.title: The structure of a formal email
content.body:
A formal email has five parts:

1. SUBJECT LINE â€” short and clear
   'Request for Leave â€” 12-14 March'

2. GREETING
   'Dear Mr Sharma,' / 'Dear Ms Priya,' / 'Dear Sir/Madam,'

3. OPENING LINE â€” state your reason
   'I am writing to request...' / 'I am writing regarding...'

4. BODY â€” your main message (1-3 short paragraphs)

5. CLOSING + SIGN-OFF
   'I look forward to hearing from you.'
   'Yours sincerely,' / 'Kind regards,' / 'Best regards,'
   [Your name]
content.grammar_note:
Formal emails use full forms: I am (not I'm), I do not (not I don't)
Avoid casual phrases: 'Hey!', 'Thanks a ton', 'Cheers'

content.title: Key formal email phrases
content.body:
OPENING LINES:
â€¢ I am writing to inform you that...
â€¢ I am writing with regard to...
â€¢ Further to our conversation, I would like to...
â€¢ I am contacting you regarding...

MAKING REQUESTS:
â€¢ I would be grateful if you could...
â€¢ Could you please...?
â€¢ I would appreciate it if...
â€¢ Please find attached...

CLOSING LINES:
â€¢ I look forward to hearing from you.
â€¢ Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.
â€¢ Thank you for your time and consideration.
â€¢ I await your response.
content.grammar_note:
Use 'would' for polite requests â€” more formal than 'can'
'I would be grateful' > 'I want'

content.title: Discussing how to write a formal email â€” college, Bangalore
content.setting: Sonal is helping her friend Arjun write a formal email to a professor requesting an extension on his assignment.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Sonal, I need to write a formal email to Professor Verma asking for an assignment extension. I've never written one before.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Okay, it's not too difficult. First, write a clear subject line â€” something like 'Request for Assignment Extension â€” Arjun Mehta'.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Okay. Then how do I start?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Start with 'Dear Professor Verma,' â€” never 'Hey' or just her first name in a formal email.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Got it. And then?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Your opening line should state why you're writing. Something like: 'I am writing to request an extension for the Marketing Case Study assignment.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Simple and to the point. What do I put in the body?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Explain your reason briefly â€” keep it honest and professional. Something like: 'I have been unwell this week and have been unable to complete the work to the required standard.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Should I mention the new date I want?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Yes â€” say exactly what you need: 'I would be grateful if you could grant me an extension until Friday, 14th March.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Perfect. And how do I end it?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: End with: 'I apologise for any inconvenience caused and I look forward to your response.' Then sign off with 'Yours sincerely' or 'Kind regards' and your full name.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: This is really helpful. Should I say anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: You could add: 'Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information.' That shows you're being professional and cooperative.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Great! I'll write it up now. Thanks so much, Sonal.

content.instruction: Read this formal email aloud and then explain each part â€” what is the subject, greeting, opening line, body, and closing?
content.prompt:
Read and explain the structure of this email:

Subject: Request for Meeting â€” Project Proposal

Dear Ms Kavitha,

I am writing to request a brief meeting to discuss my project proposal for the upcoming product launch.

I would be grateful if you could spare 20 minutes at your earliest convenience. I am available on Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon.

Please find a summary of the proposal attached.

I look forward to hearing from you. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information.

Yours sincerely,
Arjun Mehta
content.example_response: This is a well-structured formal email. The subject line is 'Request for Meeting â€” Project Proposal' â€” it's short, clear and tells the reader exactly what the email is about. The greeting is 'Dear Ms Kavitha' â€” formal and respectful, using the title and name. The opening line 'I am writing to request a brief meeting' immediately states the purpose â€” this is very important in formal emails. The body has two short paragraphs: the first makes the request and explains how long it will take, and the second offers specific availability â€” Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon â€” which makes it easy for the reader to respond. There's also a mention of an attachment. The closing lines are very professional: 'I look forward to hearing from you' is the standard formal closing, and 'please do not hesitate to contact me' shows politeness. The sign-off 'Yours sincerely' followed by full name is perfectly formal.

lessons.title: Formal vs informal email language
lessons.description: Understand and practise the difference between formal and informal register in written English.

content.title: Register â€” formal vs informal
content.body:
REGISTER means the level of formality in your language. You choose based on WHO you are writing to.

FORMAL (to a boss, client, professor, stranger, company):
â€¢ Full sentences, full forms
â€¢ No slang, no emoji
â€¢ Polite and distant
â€¢ 'I am writing to request...'

INFORMAL (to a friend, close colleague, family):
â€¢ Contractions, casual phrases
â€¢ Can use emoji, exclamation marks
â€¢ Friendly and warm
â€¢ 'Hey! Just wanted to check...'

SEMI-FORMAL (to a known colleague you're friendly with):
â€¢ Mix of both â€” friendly but professional
content.grammar_note:
Register mistakes = mismatches
Writing to your boss like a friend = too casual
Writing to your friend like a formal letter = awkward

content.title: Formal vs informal phrase comparison
content.body:
INFORMAL â†’ FORMAL

â€¢ Hey! â†’ Dear [Name],
â€¢ Just wanted to say â†’ I am writing to inform you
â€¢ Can you...? â†’ Could you please...? / I would be grateful if you could...
â€¢ Sorry for the late reply â†’ I apologise for the delay in responding
â€¢ Thanks! â†’ Thank you for your time
â€¢ No problem â†’ It is not an inconvenience
â€¢ I'll do it â†’ I will ensure it is completed
â€¢ ASAP â†’ at your earliest convenience
â€¢ FYI â†’ For your information / Please note that
â€¢ Let me know â†’ Please do not hesitate to contact me
â€¢ Bye! / Cheers â†’ Yours sincerely / Kind regards
content.grammar_note:
Abbreviations like FYI, ASAP, BTW are informal only
Never use them in formal emails

content.instruction: Compare these two situations and explain what register you would use and why. Then give 3 example phrases for each.

content.example_response:
For Situation A â€” emailing a new client â€” I would use formal register because I don't know them personally and this is a professional business context. I would use phrases like: 'Dear Mr/Ms [Name],' to open, 'I am writing to present our proposal for the upcoming project' for the opening line, and 'I look forward to your feedback at your earliest convenience' to close. I would not use contractions or abbreviations.

For Situation B â€” messaging my close colleague â€” I would use informal register. We know each other well, this is a quick update, and it's through messaging, not email. I'd write something like: 'Hey! Running 10 mins late â€” sorry!' or 'On my way â€” traffic is bad, be there by 9:15' or just 'Late by 10 â€” sorry!' with maybe a quick emoji. The context matters a lot â€” the same information is communicated very differently depending on who you're talking to.

content.next_lesson: Making appointments â€” suggesting, accepting, and confirming times

lessons.title: Making appointments
lessons.description: Learn how to suggest, accept, decline, and confirm appointments â€” by phone, email, and in conversation.

content.title: Suggesting a time â€” language for making appointments
content.body:
When making an appointment, you suggest a time and check if the other person is free.

Suggesting:
â€¢ Are you free on Tuesday at 3 PM?
â€¢ Would Wednesday morning work for you?
â€¢ How does Thursday afternoon suit you?
â€¢ Shall we say 2 o'clock on Friday?
â€¢ Could we meet sometime next week?

Accepting:
â€¢ Yes, that works for me.
â€¢ Tuesday at 3 is perfect.
â€¢ That suits me well.
â€¢ I'll put it in my calendar.

Declining politely:
â€¢ I'm afraid I'm not free then.
â€¢ That doesn't work for me â€” could we try another time?
â€¢ I'm busy that morning â€” would afternoon be possible?
content.grammar_note:
Use WOULD/COULD for polite suggestions
'Shall we say...' = very natural for appointments
'I'm afraid...' = polite way to decline

content.title: Confirming and checking appointment details
content.body:
After agreeing a time, always confirm the details clearly.

Confirming:
â€¢ So, we're meeting on Tuesday at 3 PM â€” is that correct?
â€¢ Just to confirm â€” the appointment is on Friday at 10 AM.
â€¢ I'll send you a calendar invite to confirm.
â€¢ Can I just double-check the venue?

Checking details:
â€¢ Where exactly are we meeting?
â€¢ Is it at your office or mine?
â€¢ Should I call you or will you call me?
â€¢ What's the address?

Reminding:
â€¢ Just a reminder about our meeting tomorrow at 2 PM.
â€¢ Don't forget â€” we're meeting on Thursday!
content.grammar_note:
'Just to confirm...' = polite way to double check
'Just a reminder...' = gentle notification

lessons.title: Changing and cancelling plans
lessons.description: Learn how to apologise for cancellations, explain reasons, and suggest new times â€” politely and naturally.

content.title: Cancelling plans â€” what to say
content.body:
When cancelling, you need three things:
1. Apology â€” I'm so sorry / I apologise
2. Reason â€” something has come up / I'm unwell
3. Offer to reschedule â€” can we find another time?

Formal cancellation:
â€¢ I am writing to cancel our appointment on Friday.
â€¢ I sincerely apologise for the inconvenience.
â€¢ I wonder if we could reschedule for the following week.

Informal cancellation:
â€¢ Hey, I'm really sorry but I have to cancel tomorrow.
â€¢ Something came up â€” can we move it?
â€¢ So sorry! Can we do it next week instead?
content.grammar_note:
'Something came up' = an unexpected problem appeared
'I wonder if we could...' = very polite way to suggest

content.title: Changing plans â€” rescheduling language
content.body:
When you need to change (not cancel) a plan:

â€¢ Could we move the meeting to Thursday instead?
â€¢ Would it be possible to push it back by an hour?
â€¢ I need to bring it forward â€” could we meet earlier?
â€¢ Can we reschedule for next week?
â€¢ Would you mind if we changed the time?

Key vocabulary:
â€¢ Reschedule = arrange for a different time
â€¢ Push back = move to a later time
â€¢ Bring forward = move to an earlier time
â€¢ Move = change to a different time
â€¢ Postpone = delay to a later date
content.grammar_note:
'Push back' = later
'Bring forward' = earlier
These are opposite phrasal verbs â€” easy to confuse!

Dear Mr Sharma,

I am writing to inform you that I am unfortunately unable to attend our meeting scheduled for Tuesday, 14th October at 2 PM.

Due to an urgent internal matter that requires my immediate attention, I must cancel this appointment. I sincerely apologise for the inconvenience this may cause, particularly given the short notice.

I remain very keen to meet and discuss the proposal. I wonder if we could reschedule for the following week? I am available on Tuesday 21st or Wednesday 22nd, at any time that suits you.

Once again, I apologise for this change of plan and I thank you for your understanding. I look forward to your response.

Kind regards,
Arjun Mehta
key_phrases: I am writing to inform you
I wonder if we could reschedule

content.instruction: Complete this rescheduling conversation.

Priya: Hi Ravi, I'm ___ (1) sorry but I need to ___ (2) our meeting this afternoon.
Ravi: Oh no â€” is everything okay?
Priya: Yes â€” something ___ (3) up at work. A client emergency. I ___ (4) for the short notice.
Ravi: No worries at all. Would you like to ___ (5) for later this week?
Priya: That would be great. Could we ___ (6) it ___ (7) to tomorrow at the same time?
Ravi: Tomorrow at 3 PM works for me. ___ (8) to confirm â€” tomorrow, Thursday, 3 PM at the coffee shop?
Priya: Exactly right. Thank you so much for being so ___ (9). See you then!

content.title: Lesson 10 complete â€” Changing and cancelling plans
key_points: Cancellation = apology + reason + offer to reschedule
'Something came up' = unexpected event
PUSH BACK = later time; BRING FORWARD = earlier time
POSTPONE = delay to a later date
'I apologise FOR...' â€” always use 'for'
Formal cancellations need full sentences; informal can be briefer but still warm

lessons.title: Giving directions in writing
lessons.description: Write clear directions in emails and messages using imperative verbs, prepositions of movement, and landmark language.

content.title: Imperatives for directions
content.body:
When giving written directions, use IMPERATIVE verbs â€” the base form of the verb.

Basic direction verbs:
â€¢ Turn left / Turn right
â€¢ Go straight ahead / Continue straight
â€¢ Take the first / second / third turning
â€¢ Cross the road / cross at the signal
â€¢ Go past the temple / go past the petrol station
â€¢ Walk along the main road
â€¢ Go through the gate / through the underpass
â€¢ Head towards the flyover
â€¢ Keep going until you reach...
â€¢ You will see... / You can't miss it!

Distance and time:
â€¢ It's about 200 metres / about 5 minutes on foot.
content.grammar_note:
Imperatives use base verb â€” no subject needed
'Turn left' NOT 'You should turn left' (though both are possible)
Written directions use numbers: 'Take the 2nd right'

content.title: Prepositions of movement
content.body:
These prepositions describe how you move through space:

â€¢ ALONG â€” move in the same direction as a road
  'Walk along MG Road for 200 metres.'

â€¢ PAST â€” go further than a landmark without stopping
  'Go past the Apollo hospital.'

â€¢ THROUGH â€” move from one side to the other
  'Go through the main gate.'

â€¢ ACROSS â€” cross from one side to the other
  'Walk across the bridge.'

â€¢ TOWARDS â€” in the direction of something
  'Head towards the railway station.'

â€¢ INTO â€” enter a place
  'Turn into the lane on your left.'
content.grammar_note:
along = direction
past = movement beyond a point
through = from side to side
across = from one side to the other

vocabulary.meaning: a well-known place used as a reference point
vocabulary.example: The temple is a useful landmark.

vocabulary.meaning: where two or more roads meet
vocabulary.example: Turn left at the junction.

vocabulary.meaning: a bridge that goes over a road
vocabulary.example: Go under the flyover and take a right.
At the roundabout, take the third exit.
We're opposite the post office.
The parking is adjacent to the building.

sentences: Walk along the main road for about 300 metres.
Turn left at the traffic signal near the petrol station.
Go past the bus stand and take the second right.
Go through the gate â€” the office is on the ground floor.
You can't miss it â€” it's a large blue building on the corner.

content.question: Which preposition correctly completes: 'Walk ___ the bridge and turn right.'?
content.correct: across
content.explanation: ACROSS = move from one side to the other. A bridge goes across water â€” you walk 'across' it.

content.question: Which sentence gives the clearest written direction?
options: It's somewhere near a temple, I think.
Take the second left after the petrol station â€” the building is on your right, opposite the post office.
You'll find it if you look.
Near the temple, go somewhere and then right.
content.correct: Take the second left after the petrol station â€” the building is on your right, opposite the post office.
content.explanation: Good written directions: specific landmarks + numbered turns + final location.

content.question: Complete: 'Go ___ the main road until you reach the railway station.'
content.correct: along
content.explanation: ALONG = travel in the same direction as a road. You go 'along' a road.

content.question: What does 'You can't miss it!' mean in directions?
options: The place is very far away.
The place is hidden and hard to find.
The place is very obvious and easy to spot.
content.correct: The place is very obvious and easy to spot.
content.explanation: 'You can't miss it!' = it's very visible and easy to find â€” a common reassurance in directions.

content.title: Sending directions in a message â€” Chennai
content.setting: Priya is hosting a housewarming party. Her friend Ankit from out of town asks for directions from Chennai Central station to her flat.

dialogue.speaker: Ankit
dialogue.text: Priya! So excited about the housewarming. Can you send me directions from Chennai Central? I'll be coming by auto.

dialogue.speaker: Priya

dialogue.speaker: Ankit

dialogue.speaker: Priya

dialogue.speaker: Ankit

dialogue.speaker: Priya

dialogue.speaker: Priya

content.instruction: Give written-style directions from a well-known location to your home, college, or office. Use landmarks, imperative verbs, and prepositions of movement.
content.prompt: Give directions from a local landmark to a place you know well. Use at least 6 direction steps.
content.example_response: Here are directions from Besant Nagar beach to my flat in Chennai. Exit the beach from the main entrance and head towards the Elliot's Beach road. Walk along the beach road going north for about 300 metres. Go past the popular Ratna CafÃ© on your right â€” don't turn there. Continue straight until you reach a signal with a petrol station on the left corner. Turn left at that signal. Walk along that road for about 150 metres and go past a small park. After the park, take the first right â€” it's a quiet lane. Go through the white metal gate at the end of the lane. My building, Sunrise Apartments, is straight ahead â€” it's a four-storey pink building, you can't miss it. I'm on the second floor, Flat 2A. The whole walk takes about 10 minutes from the beach.

content.instruction: A friend is visiting your city for the first time. They are at the main railway station. Give them step-by-step written directions to a famous local restaurant or attraction you like.

content.example_response: Hey! So glad you're finally in Chennai! Here are the directions from Chennai Central to Saravana Bhavan on Usman Road â€” it's one of the best places for South Indian food! From Chennai Central station, take the main exit and head towards the Anna Salai road â€” that's the wide main road facing you. Walk along Anna Salai going south for about 600 metres. You'll pass a big LIC building on your right â€” keep going. At the big roundabout, take the second exit â€” that puts you on the road towards T. Nagar. Go along that road past the Panagal Park bus stop and continue until you see a big intersection. Turn right at that intersection â€” you're now on Usman Road. Walk along Usman Road for about 200 metres. Saravana Bhavan is on the left side â€” it's a large restaurant with green and white signage, you absolutely can't miss it. Total walking time from the station: about 20 minutes. Or just take an auto and show the driver 'Saravana Bhavan, Usman Road, T. Nagar' â€” everyone knows it!

content.title: Lesson 11 complete â€” Giving directions in writing
key_points: Use IMPERATIVE verbs for directions: Turn, Go, Walk, Take, Head
ALONG = travel the length of a road
PAST = move further than a landmark
THROUGH = from one side to the other (gates, tunnels)
ACROSS = from one side to the other (bridges, roads)
TOWARDS = in the direction of something
Use landmarks + numbered turns + distance/time estimates for clear directions

lessons.title: Plans, scheduling and calendars
lessons.description: Talk about schedules, priorities, and time management using a range of future and planning expressions.

content.title: Talking about schedules
content.body:
Useful language for discussing schedules and calendars:

Describing what's planned:
â€¢ I have a meeting first thing in the morning.
â€¢ The presentation is scheduled for 2 PM.
â€¢ I've got a packed day tomorrow.
â€¢ My afternoon is free / blocked / taken.

Asking about schedule:
â€¢ Are you free at any point this week?
â€¢ Do you have a window on Thursday?
â€¢ When are you next available?
â€¢ Is your morning free or do you have something?

Priority language:
â€¢ My top priority this week is...
â€¢ The most urgent thing is...
â€¢ After that, I'll focus on...
â€¢ I need to fit this in before Friday.
content.grammar_note:
'Have a window' = have a free time slot
'Packed day' = very busy day with no free time
'First thing' = very early in the morning

content.title: Time management expressions
content.body:
Useful time and planning expressions:

â€¢ I'm tied up until 3 PM. (busy)
â€¢ I'm snowed under this week. (overwhelmed with work)
â€¢ I need to block some time for this. (reserve time)
â€¢ Let me check my calendar and get back to you.
â€¢ I'll pencil you in for Tuesday. (tentative booking)
â€¢ Can we move this to the next slot?
â€¢ I've overbooked myself this week!
â€¢ I like to plan ahead / I tend to leave things to the last minute.
â€¢ Deadline pressure really motivates me.
content.grammar_note:
'Tied up' = busy, unavailable
'Snowed under' = overwhelmed
'Block time' = reserve a specific time for a task
'Pencil in' = put in schedule but not final

vocabulary.meaning: the last time by which something must be done
vocabulary.example: The report deadline is Friday at 5 PM.

vocabulary.meaning: the most important thing to do first
vocabulary.example: My top priority is finishing the presentation.

vocabulary.meaning: a plan for when things will happen
vocabulary.example: Check the schedule and find a free slot.

vocabulary.meaning: the total amount of work to be done
vocabulary.example: My workload is very heavy this month.

vocabulary.meaning: not done by the expected time
vocabulary.example: This report is already overdue.

vocabulary.meaning: to decide how much time or resources to give to something
vocabulary.example: I need to allocate more time to this project.

sentences: I've got a packed day tomorrow â€” back-to-back meetings until 5.
Do you have a window on Friday afternoon?
My top priority this week is the client proposal.
I'm tied up until 3 but free after that.
Let me check my calendar and get back to you.

content.question: Priya says: 'I'm completely snowed under this week.' What does she mean?
options: She is on a winter holiday.
She has nothing to do this week.
She is overwhelmed with work and very busy.
content.correct: She is overwhelmed with work and very busy.
content.explanation: 'Snowed under' = informal expression for being overwhelmed with work.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I'll pencil you in for Thursday.' What does this mean?
options: He will write the plan in pencil only.
He will put you in his schedule tentatively â€” it's not completely confirmed yet.
He will cancel all Thursday plans.
He will call you on Thursday.
content.correct: He will put you in his schedule tentatively â€” it's not completely confirmed yet.
content.explanation: 'Pencil in' = a tentative plan â€” like writing in pencil because it can be erased (changed).

content.question: How do you ask someone if they have a free time slot this week?
options: Do you have a window anywhere this week?
Have you got nothing to do?
content.correct: Do you have a window anywhere this week?
content.explanation: 'Do you have a window?' = professional way to ask if someone has a free slot.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'tied up'?
options: I'm tied up on Wednesday morning â€” I have three meetings back to back.
I tied up my calendar yesterday.
My schedule is tied up empty.
content.correct: I'm tied up on Wednesday morning â€” I have three meetings back to back.
content.explanation: 'Tied up' = unavailable/busy. 'I'm tied up on [day/time]' is the natural structure.

content.title: Weekly planning conversation â€” startup office, Bengaluru
content.setting: Kavya (team lead) is planning the week with her colleague Arjun on Monday morning.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Morning Arjun! Crazy week ahead â€” let's do a quick schedule check.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes, I already feel snowed under and it's only Monday!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! So, my top priority this week is the investor deck â€” that's due Friday. What's yours?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Finishing the API integration â€” it's already a day overdue. I need to block at least 6 hours for that.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Absolutely. Do you have a window for a sync with the design team? They need 30 minutes with you.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'm tied up Tuesday morning â€” back-to-back calls. But I have a window on Wednesday from 11 to 12.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Perfect. I'll pencil you in for Wednesday at 11 with the design team.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: And the client call this Thursday â€” is that still on?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, 3 PM Thursday is confirmed. I'd suggest preparing your technical summary by Wednesday evening at the latest.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'll allocate Thursday morning to the prep. I work better first thing anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Good plan. Also, team lunch on Friday â€” that's scheduled for 1 PM at the usual place. It won't take more than an hour.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I've got it in my calendar. Looks like a packed week but manageable.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Exactly. Let's check in again on Wednesday to see where we are. Does that work?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Wednesday morning â€” 9:30 AM? That suits me perfectly.

Why is Arjun tied up on Tuesday morning?

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: I'm very snowed in with work this week.
sentences.correct: I'm very snowed under with work this week.
sentences.explanation: The correct idiom is 'snowed UNDER' (overwhelmed), not 'snowed in' (which means trapped inside due to snow).

sentences.wrong: Do you have a window on Thursday morning free?
sentences.correct: Do you have a window on Thursday morning?
sentences.explanation: 'Do you have a window' already implies a free slot â€” 'free' is redundant.

sentences.wrong: I'll pen you in for Friday.
sentences.correct: I'll pencil you in for Friday.
sentences.explanation: The correct expression is 'PENCIL in' â€” not 'pen in'. Pencil implies it can be changed.

content.scenario: Your manager asks if you can attend an optional training session on Thursday. Your Thursday is already packed with two client calls and a deadline. What do you say?
options: I'm afraid Thursday is completely packed â€” I have two client calls and a project deadline. Is the training available on another day?
Thursday may be okay I suppose.
content.correct: I'm afraid Thursday is completely packed â€” I have two client calls and a project deadline. Is the training available on another day?
content.explanation: Honest, professional response using 'I'm afraid' + clear reason + constructive alternative question.

content.instruction: Describe your ideal productive week. What would your schedule look like? What would be your top priorities? Use the scheduling language from this lesson.
content.prompt: Describe your ideal work or study week â€” what is scheduled, what your priorities are, and how you plan your time.
content.example_response: If I could design my ideal productive week, here's how it would look. My top priority on Monday would be to tackle the most difficult task of the week first thing in the morning â€” when my energy is highest. I'd block two to three hours for deep, focused work before any meetings start. I'd schedule all team meetings on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons â€” that way Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings stay free for actual work. I don't like having a packed day with no breathing room, so I'd always leave at least a 30-minute window between tasks. Wednesday would be my creative day â€” I'd allocate time for brainstorming and planning future projects. I'd make sure my Friday afternoon is relatively free so I can wrap up any overdue tasks and plan for the following week. And I absolutely would not leave anything overdue â€” I find that deadline pressure stresses me out, so I prefer to work ahead.

content.instruction: Describe a time when you were completely snowed under with work or study. What was the situation? How did you manage? What would you do differently?
content.prompt: Tell a story about a very hectic, overloaded week using scheduling and time management vocabulary.
content.example_response: Last December, just before our company's year-end deadline, I was completely snowed under. My workload was absolutely unmanageable â€” I had three major reports that were all overdue, a product demo scheduled for a key client, and my manager had added two more tasks to my list without removing anything else. I was tied up from 8 AM to 9 PM every day. I didn't have a single window in my day and I was surviving on coffee. What I should have done differently: I should have blocked time earlier in the month for those reports instead of leaving them to the last minute. I should also have been honest with my manager about my workload â€” I was trying to do everything but doing nothing properly. The lesson I learned was to always allocate time proactively, be transparent about capacity, and never agree to take on more when you're already at maximum. Now I always keep at least one buffer hour in my day â€” I never fully pack my schedule.

content.instruction: Complete Kavya's weekly planning email to her team.
content.text:
Hi team,

Here's a quick overview of our ___ (1) for this week:

TUESDAY: I'm completely ___ (4) up in the morning with back-to-back calls. However, I have a ___ (5) between 2 and 3 PM for any quick discussions.

WEDNESDAY: I've ___ (6) out 10 AMâ€“12 PM for the campaign planning session â€” please don't schedule anything in that time.

THURSDAY: Client presentation at 3 PM â€” everyone involved, please ___ (7) this as your top task for the day.

FRIDAY: Team lunch at 1 PM â€” looking forward to it! I'll ___ (8) you all in once I confirm the venue.

Please check your own ___ (9) and let me know if there are any conflicts. It looks like a ___ (10) week â€” let's stay focused!

content.title: Lesson 12 complete â€” Plans, scheduling and calendars
key_points: 'Snowed under' = overwhelmed with work
'Tied up' = busy, unavailable
'Do you have a window?' = do you have a free slot?
'Pencil in' = tentative booking
'Block time' = reserve time for a specific task
'Top priority' = most important task
'Packed day' = very busy day with no free time
content.next_lesson: Unit 10 Review and A2 Exit Practice

lessons.title: Unit 10 Review + A2 Exit Practice
lessons.description: A comprehensive review of all Unit 10 grammar, vocabulary, and communication skills, plus extended A2-level speaking practice.

content.title: Unit 10 â€” Complete grammar summary
content.body:
All grammar covered in Unit 10:

1. PRESENT CONTINUOUS for future: fixed, arranged plans
   'I'm meeting her at 6 tonight.'

2. GOING TO: decided plans + evidence predictions
   'I'm going to apply.' / 'It's going to rain!'

3. WILL: predictions, spontaneous decisions, offers, promises
   'I think it'll rain.' / 'I'll get it!' / 'I promise I'll call.'

4. MAY / MIGHT: possibility, not certain
   'She might come.' / 'It may rain.'

5. HEDGING: I think, probably, as far as I know, I suppose

6. FORMAL EMAILS: structure + language

7. APPOINTMENTS: suggest / accept / decline / confirm

8. CANCELLATIONS: apologise + reason + reschedule

9. DIRECTIONS: imperatives + prepositions of movement

10. SCHEDULING: snowed under, tied up, window, pencil in

content.title: The three futures â€” final comparison
content.body:
Quick decision guide for the three futures:

Is it a FIXED APPOINTMENT? (booked, specific date/time)
â†’ PRESENT CONTINUOUS
'We're meeting at 3 on Thursday.'

Did you DECIDE BEFORE speaking? OR visible evidence?
â†’ GOING TO
'I'm going to study medicine.' / 'It's going to fall!'

Did you decide JUST NOW? OR general prediction/offer/promise?
â†’ WILL
'I'll get the door!' / 'India will grow.' / 'I'll call you.'

NOT SURE if it will happen?
â†’ MAY / MIGHT
'She might come.' / 'It may rain.'

vocabulary.meaning: plan and fix something in advance
vocabulary.example: We arranged to meet on Friday.
Can we reschedule the meeting?
The event was postponed.

vocabulary.meaning: free time; when you are free
vocabulary.example: What's your availability next week?
I am writing regarding your application.
Yours sincerely, Priya Rajan
My top priority is the presentation.
I'm uncertain about the schedule.
Use the temple as a landmark.

sentences: I'm presenting to the board at 10 AM on Thursday â€” it's in the calendar.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses the PRESENT CONTINUOUS for a future arrangement?
options: I will have a meeting tomorrow.
I'm going to have a meeting.
I'm having a meeting with the CEO at 10 AM tomorrow â€” it's confirmed.
content.correct: I'm having a meeting with the CEO at 10 AM tomorrow â€” it's confirmed.
content.explanation: Fixed, confirmed plan with specific time = present continuous for future.

content.question: Your glass of water is right at the edge of the table and looks like it's about to fall. What do you say?
options: The glass is going to fall!
The glass might fall at some point.
content.correct: The glass is going to fall!
content.explanation: Visible, immediate evidence = GOING TO prediction. You can see it about to fall right now.

content.question: Which closing line belongs in a FORMAL email?
options: I look forward to hearing from you. Kind regards,
content.correct: I look forward to hearing from you. Kind regards,
content.explanation: Standard formal closing = 'I look forward to hearing from you' + professional sign-off.

content.question: The meeting is at 2 PM. You need it to be at 3 PM instead (later). What do you say?
options: Could we bring the meeting forward to 3 PM?
Could we push the meeting back to 3 PM?
Could we postpone the meeting to 1 PM?
Could we bring the meeting backwards to 3 PM?
content.correct: Could we push the meeting back to 3 PM?
content.explanation: PUSH BACK = move to a later time. 3 PM is later than 2 PM.

content.title: Unit 10 review dialogue â€” Full professional conversation
content.setting: Arjun (startup founder) and Meera (potential investor) are wrapping up an initial call and planning next steps.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Arjun, I've really enjoyed learning about your product today. I think it has strong potential.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Thank you so much, Meera. I'm glad you see the value in it. We're going to launch the full version in March.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: March â€” that's ambitious. Are you on track?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: We might push it back by two weeks if the testing phase reveals issues, but as of now, March 15th is the target.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I see. I'll probably want to see the technical details before making any commitment. Could we arrange a follow-up?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Absolutely. I'll send you a formal proposal by Friday. I'm writing it up this week.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: That works. As for a meeting â€” would early next week suit you? I'm tied up all of this week.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Of course. Would Tuesday or Wednesday morning work for you?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I'm afraid Tuesday is packed â€” I have board meetings all day. Wednesday at 10 AM works perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Wednesday 10 AM â€” wonderful. Shall we meet at your office, or would you prefer a video call?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Let's do it in person â€” I find it easier to have these conversations face to face.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Agreed. Just to confirm â€” Wednesday, 10 AM, your office. I'll bring printed copies of the proposal.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Perfect. I look forward to it. I'll send you a calendar invite shortly.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Thank you so much, Meera. I'll have the proposal in your inbox by Friday. Have a great day!

Why can't Meera meet on Tuesday?

content.instruction: Mixed Unit 10 error correction â€” 3 sentences with mistakes.

sentences.wrong: I'm going to will travel to Goa next month.
sentences.correct: I'm going to travel to Goa next month.
sentences.explanation: Don't combine GOING TO and WILL â€” use one. 'I'm going to travel' or 'I'll travel' â€” not both.

sentences.wrong: I apologise by the short notice.
sentences.correct: I apologise for the short notice.
sentences.explanation: APOLOGISE FOR â€” not 'apologise by'.

sentences.wrong: Go across the road for 500 metres, then turn right.
sentences.correct: Go along the road for 500 metres, then turn right.
sentences.explanation: ALONG = travel in the same direction as a road. ACROSS = from one side to the other.

content.scenario: You receive an email from a company you applied to â€” they've invited you to an interview. You want to confirm attendance and ask about what to bring. Which is the best reply?
options: Yes! I'll be there. What do I need?

Dear [Name], Thank you for your email. I am delighted to confirm my attendance for the interview on [date] at [time]. Could you please advise what documents I should bring? I look forward to meeting you. Kind regards, [Your name]
Hey! So excited â€” I'll come. What do I bring? Thanks ðŸ˜Š
content.correct: Dear [Name], Thank you for your email. I am delighted to confirm my attendance for the interview on [date] at [time]. Could you please advise what documents I should bring? I look forward to meeting you. Kind regards, [Your name]
content.explanation: A company email = formal register. This response confirms clearly, asks a polite question, and closes professionally.

content.instruction: A2 Exit Speaking Task 1: Talk for 90 seconds about your plans for the next 3 months. Use all three future forms (will, going to, present continuous) at least once each, plus may/might for uncertainty.
content.prompt: Describe your plans for the next 3 months using all future forms: will, going to, present continuous, and may/might.
content.example_response: For the next three months, I have quite a mix of fixed plans, decisions, and hopes. Starting with what's already arranged: I'm attending my colleague's wedding on the 20th of this month â€” it's fully confirmed and I've already RSVP'd. I'm also presenting at a department meeting on the 5th of next month. Moving on to decisions I've already made: I'm going to enrol in an advanced Excel course in January â€” I've been meaning to do this for a while. I'm also going to start saving more systematically â€” I've already opened a new account for it. As for predictions: I think the project I'm leading will probably be completed by March. I believe the team will do well â€” everyone is very motivated. And for the uncertain parts: I might visit my grandparents in February if I can get leave approved â€” I'm not sure yet because my manager hasn't confirmed it. There's also a chance I may get a chance to travel for work to Delhi in March â€” but nothing is decided. So it's a mix of fixed things, solid decisions, and exciting possibilities!

content.title: UNIT 10 COMPLETE â€” Future, plans & communication
key_points: Present continuous: fixed future arrangements with specific time
Going to: decided plans + evidence predictions
Will: general predictions, spontaneous decisions, offers, promises
May/might: possibility and uncertainty (not 'will maybe')
Hedging: I think, probably, as far as I know, I suppose
Formal emails: 5 parts â€” subject, greeting, opening, body, closing
Appointments: suggest â†’ accept/decline â†’ confirm
Cancellations: apologise + reason + reschedule
Directions: imperatives + along/past/through/across/towards
Scheduling: snowed under, tied up, window, pencil in, top priority
